clinicalProblem.clinicalIntent: Exclude white-coat effect as a cause of pseudoresistance in adults with apparent treatment-resistant hypertension
clinicalProblem.whenToUse: When office BP readings suggest treatment-resistant hypertension despite appropriate medication regimen
clinicalProblem.decisionOrAction: Recommend out-of-office BP monitoring to differentiate true resistance from white-coat effect

navigationPath.purpose: Entry point for treatment-resistant hypertension evaluation
Confirm apparent treatment-resistant hypertension based on office BP
Introduce white-coat effect as possible cause
Select out-of-office monitoring method (ABPM or HBPM)
Provide patient instructions for out-of-office monitoring
Determine true resistance vs white-coat effect

validationRules: Medication regimen optimized and adherence confirmed

rules: Confirm apparent treatment-resistant hypertension (office BP above target despite ≥3 medications including diuretic)
Exclude other causes of pseudoresistance (medication non-adherence, improper technique, etc.)
Obtain out-of-office BP monitoring (ABPM preferred or HBPM)
Compare office BP to out-of-office BP averages
If out-of-office BP is significantly lower than office BP → white-coat effect
If out-of-office BP remains elevated → true treatment-resistant hypertension

categories.name: White-Coat Effect
categories.definition: Elevated office BP with normal out-of-office BP
categories.action: Continue current medications, avoid unnecessary intensification

categories.name: True Treatment-Resistant Hypertension
categories.action: Consider additional medications or specialized referral

escalationLogic.ifAnySymptomPresent: Do not proceed with white-coat evaluation, send to ER immediately
escalationLogic.ifNoSymptoms: Proceed with out-of-office monitoring for white-coat exclusion

appDisplay.screenType: Standard clinical decision support with crisis override
appDisplay.button: Standard navigation with crisis alert available
appDisplay.behavior: Screen for crisis symptoms first, then proceed

nextStepRouting.providerSuggestion: If white-coat effect confirmed: continue current regimen, avoid medication escalation. If true resistance: consider adding mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist or refer to specialist
nextStepRouting.followUpTiming: Repeat out-of-office monitoring in 3-6 months or if office BP changes
nextStepRouting.repeatMeasurement: Confirm with repeat out-of-office monitoring annually or with medication changes
nextStepRouting.navigatorEscalation: Flag true treatment-resistant hypertension for care team review
nextStepRouting.documentation: Log office vs out-of-office BP comparison to health record
